title: An open letter to my city council, about benches
summary: A resident writes a calm, specific complaint about a recent design decision: the addition of armrests to the middle of public benches.
body:
To the Honourable Members of the District Council,

I write about a small thing. The new benches on Ly Thuong Kiet Street were installed this year with metal armrests bolted into the middle of each seat. The change was presented as a modernisation. I would like to explain what was removed when those armrests were added.

A bench without a centre armrest is several things at once. It is a seat for a tired worker on his lunch break. It is a place where two friends can sit close enough to speak quietly. It is, for the man who sleeps on it, a bed of last resort. It is a perch for a grandmother resting between the market and her apartment. It is, in short, a public good that does not demand of its user any single posture.

The new armrests reduce the bench to one thing: a seat for a person alone, upright, and not going to lie down. The armrests were not added to make sitting more comfortable; most of us prefer to sit unobstructed. They were added because the council, after pressure from a small group of complainants, wished to prevent the bench from being used by people sleeping rough.

The new bench design does not solve homelessness. It only removes the bench's other uses, in order to deny it to one group of people. The man without a home now sleeps further down the street, in a doorway. The bench has not improved his life. It has stopped serving everyone else.

If the council's goal were genuinely the well-being of rough sleepers, the response would not be a metal bar bolted into a bench. It would be shelter beds, social workers, addiction treatment, and pathways to stable housing. A bench modification is cheap and immediate — but a cheap response that does not solve the problem is not a solution. It is a redistribution of the problem onto the next surface.

I ask three things of the council. First, that you publish a clear estimate of how much was spent on these armrests across the district. Second, that you make available the consultation record showing which residents were asked and what they said. Third, that you commit to evaluating, within twelve months, whether rough sleeping in the affected streets has actually declined or has merely moved. If the second is true — and I suspect it will be — the armrests must come off.

A city is the sum of its small concessions to its residents. A bench is one of the most generous objects a city makes. To break it deliberately, to exclude its poorest user, is to make the city smaller for everyone else. I urge the council to remember that the people who sit on benches do not, mostly, lie on them. But they do, sometimes, sit two together — and the new bench does not allow even that.

Yours respectfully,
Nguyen Thi Hoa
Long-term resident, Ly Thuong Kiet Ward

annotations.meaningVi: biên bản tham vấn cộng đồng
sự nhượng bộ / sự dành cho

grammarNotes.title: Subjunctive and conditional for policy argument
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Letter dùng nhiều cấu trúc giả định để chỉ ra điều "đáng lẽ ra". 'If the council's goal were genuinely the well-being of rough sleepers, the response would not be a metal bar' — Câu điều kiện loại 2 với 'were' (subjunctive) cho giả định trái thực tế. 'I urge the council to remember…' — cấu trúc 'urge + O + to V' để đưa ra đề nghị mạnh nhưng lịch sự.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B2 letters use subjunctive and second conditional to argue counterfactually. 'If the council's goal were genuinely the well-being of rough sleepers, the response would not be a metal bar.' Note 'were' (subjunctive) rather than 'was'. The construction 'I urge the council to remember…' is a strong but polite request used in formal correspondence.

translationVi:
Kính gửi Quý Hội đồng Quận,

Tôi viết về một chuyện nhỏ. Các băng ghế mới trên phố Lý Thường Kiệt được lắp đặt năm nay với những tay vịn kim loại bắt chặt vào giữa mỗi chỗ ngồi. Thay đổi này được giới thiệu như một sự hiện đại hoá. Tôi muốn nói rõ điều gì đã bị lấy đi khi những tay vịn ấy được thêm vào.

Một băng ghế không có tay vịn giữa là nhiều thứ cùng lúc. Nó là chỗ ngồi cho một người công nhân mệt mỏi trong giờ ăn trưa. Nó là nơi hai người bạn có thể ngồi đủ gần để trò chuyện khẽ. Nó là, đối với một người ngủ trên đó, một chiếc giường cuối cùng. Nó là một chỗ tựa cho một bà cụ nghỉ giữa chợ và căn hộ. Nói tóm lại, nó là một lợi ích công không đòi hỏi người dùng phải có một tư thế duy nhất nào.

Các tay vịn mới rút băng ghế xuống chỉ còn một thứ: một chỗ ngồi cho người đơn độc, ngồi thẳng, và không nằm xuống. Tay vịn không được thêm vào để ngồi thoải mái hơn; đa số chúng ta thích ngồi không vướng. Chúng được thêm vào vì hội đồng, sau áp lực từ một nhóm nhỏ người than phiền, muốn ngăn việc băng ghế bị dùng cho người ngủ đường.

Thiết kế băng ghế mới không giải quyết được vấn đề vô gia cư. Nó chỉ loại bỏ các công dụng khác của băng ghế, để từ chối nó với một nhóm người. Người đàn ông không nhà giờ ngủ xa hơn dưới phố, trong một ô cửa nhà. Băng ghế không cải thiện đời ông. Nó cũng đã thôi phục vụ tất cả những người khác.

Nếu mục tiêu của hội đồng thật sự là phúc lợi của người ngủ đường, phản ứng sẽ không phải là một thanh kim loại bắt vào băng ghế. Nó sẽ là chỗ ngủ tạm trú, nhân viên xã hội, điều trị nghiện, và lộ trình hướng tới chỗ ở ổn định. Sửa băng ghế thì rẻ và tức thì — nhưng một phản ứng rẻ mà không giải quyết được vấn đề thì không phải là giải pháp. Nó là sự phân bổ lại vấn đề lên bề mặt kế tiếp.

Tôi đề nghị ba điều với hội đồng. Thứ nhất, công bố một ước tính rõ ràng về số tiền đã chi cho các tay vịn này trên toàn quận. Thứ hai, công khai biên bản tham vấn cho thấy đã hỏi cư dân nào và họ đã nói gì. Thứ ba, cam kết đánh giá, trong vòng mười hai tháng, liệu việc ngủ đường ở những con phố bị ảnh hưởng có thực sự giảm hay chỉ đơn giản là đã di chuyển. Nếu điều thứ hai đúng — và tôi ngờ là đúng — các tay vịn phải được tháo ra.

Một thành phố là tổng của những nhượng bộ nhỏ mà nó dành cho cư dân của mình. Một băng ghế là một trong những đồ vật hào phóng nhất mà một thành phố làm ra. Phá nó một cách cố ý, để loại trừ người dùng nghèo nhất, là làm cho thành phố nhỏ lại với tất cả những người khác. Tôi kêu gọi hội đồng nhớ rằng đa số người ngồi trên băng ghế không nằm trên đó. Nhưng đôi khi họ ngồi hai người chung — và thiết kế băng ghế mới không cho phép ngay cả điều đó.

Trân trọng,
Nguyễn Thị Hoa
Cư dân lâu năm, Phường Lý Thường Kiệt

questions.prompt: What specific change has been made to the benches on Ly Thuong Kiet Street?
options: Metal armrests have been bolted into the middle of each seat.
The benches have been painted a new colour.
Lights have been added to each bench.
questions.explanation: 'New benches… installed with metal armrests bolted into the middle of each seat.'

questions.prompt: According to the writer, what is a bench without a centre armrest?
options: A public good that does not demand any single posture of its user.
questions.explanation: 'A public good that does not demand of its user any single posture.'

questions.prompt: Why were the armrests actually added, according to the writer?
options: To prevent rough sleepers from using the benches, after pressure from a small group of complainants.
To make the benches more comfortable.
To match a national design standard.
To protect the benches from weather damage.
questions.explanation: 'They were added because the council… wished to prevent the bench from being used by people sleeping rough.'

questions.prompt: What does the writer say the new bench design does not solve?
questions.explanation: 'The new bench design does not solve homelessness.'

questions.prompt: What happens to 'the man without a home' after the redesign, according to the writer?
options: He sleeps further down the street, in a doorway.
He sleeps under the bench instead.
questions.explanation: 'The man without a home now sleeps further down the street, in a doorway.'

questions.prompt: What would a genuine response to rough sleeping look like, according to the writer?
options: Shelter beds, social workers, addiction treatment, and pathways to stable housing.
More metal bars on more benches.
Higher fines for sleeping in public.

questions.prompt: How does the writer characterise the council's bench modification?
options: Cheap and immediate, but a redistribution of the problem rather than a solution.
questions.explanation: 'A cheap response that does not solve the problem is not a solution. It is a redistribution of the problem onto the next surface.'

questions.prompt: How many things does the writer ask of the council?
questions.explanation: 'I ask three things of the council.'

options: Publish a clear estimate of how much was spent on the armrests across the district.
questions.explanation: 'First, that you publish… a clear estimate of how much was spent on these armrests.'

questions.prompt: Under what condition does the writer say the armrests 'must come off'?
options: If, within twelve months, rough sleeping has not declined but only moved.
If a new council is elected.
questions.explanation: 'Whether rough sleeping in the affected streets has actually declined or has merely moved. If the second is true… the armrests must come off.'

questions.prompt: What does the writer mean by 'A city is the sum of its small concessions to its residents'?
options: A city's quality lies in many small acts of generosity such as benches that allow multiple uses.
Cities are made of concrete and steel.
questions.explanation: The line generalises the bench example into a principle about urban life.

questions.prompt: What does the writer point out about the small final fact — 'they do, sometimes, sit two together'?
options: The new bench prevents even ordinary uses, not just the one the council wanted to prevent.
Most people prefer to sit alone.
questions.explanation: 'The new bench does not allow even that.'

questions.prompt: Which of these is the writer NOT claiming?
options: That rough sleepers should be allowed to sleep anywhere they like.
That the bench redesign has not solved homelessness.
That a genuine response would be expensive and slow.
That public objects should serve multiple uses.
questions.explanation: The letter does not endorse open-ended rough sleeping; it argues for substantive intervention (shelters, social workers) instead of cosmetic exclusion.
questions.hint: Look at what the writer proposes in paragraph 5.

questions.prompt: Which paraphrase best captures the writer's argument?
options: Cosmetic exclusion creates the appearance of solving a problem while actually shifting it; the cost falls on everyone who used the object for any other reason.
All public infrastructure should remain unchanged.
questions.explanation: The writer's main thesis combines the redistribution claim with the social cost to other users.

questions.prompt: How does the letter's tone best read?
options: Polite, specific, and uncomplaining — making a structural argument with concrete asks.
Furious and rhetorical, demanding immediate resignation.
Sentimental and nostalgic about old benches.
questions.explanation: The writer concedes the council's possible motivations and ends with three concrete, evaluable requests rather than threats.
questions.hint: Look at the structure of the three requests near the end.

To the Honourable Members of the District Council,

I write about a small thing. The new benches on Ly Thuong Kiet Street were installed this year with metal {{b1}} bolted into the middle of each seat. The change was presented as a {{b2}}. I would like to explain what was removed when those armrests were added.

It is, in short, a public {{b4}} that does not demand of its user any single posture.

The new armrests reduce the bench to one thing: a seat for a person alone, upright, and not going to lie down. The armrests were not added to make sitting more comfortable; most of us prefer to sit {{b5}}. They were added because the council, after pressure from a small group of complainants, wished to prevent the bench from being used by people sleeping {{b6}}.

The new bench design does not solve {{b7}}. It only removes the bench's other uses, in order to deny it to one group of people. The man without a home now sleeps further down the street, in a {{b8}}. The bench has not improved his life. It has stopped serving everyone else.

If the council's goal were genuinely the well-being of rough sleepers, the response would not be a metal bar bolted into a bench. It would be shelter beds, social workers, addiction treatment, and pathways to stable {{b9}}. A bench modification is cheap and immediate — but a cheap response that does not solve the problem is not a solution. It is a {{b10}} of the problem onto the next surface.

I ask three things of the council. First, that you publish a clear estimate of how much was spent on these armrests across the district. Second, that you make available the {{b11}} record showing which residents were asked and what they said. Third, that you commit to evaluating, within twelve months, whether rough sleeping in the affected streets has actually declined or has merely moved. If the second is true — and I suspect it will be — the armrests must come {{b12}}.

To break it deliberately, to exclude its poorest user, is to make the city {{b14}} for everyone else. I urge the council to remember that the people who sit on benches do not, mostly, lie on them. But they do, sometimes, sit {{b15}} together — and the new bench does not allow even that.

blanks.explanation: Metal armrests were bolted into the seats.
Most of us prefer to sit unobstructed — i.e. without an armrest blocking the middle.
People sleeping rough — homeless on the streets.
The bench design does not solve homelessness.
It makes the city smaller for everyone else.
Two together — the new bench prevents this.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer argues that a single design choice — adding an armrest — reduces a bench from many uses to one. Where in your own neighbourhood has a public object been quietly redesigned to exclude a particular kind of user? What other uses were also lost in the process?
